data.title: Northpointe signals $4.1B–$4.3B MPP loan growth target for 2026 as digital deposit strategies expand
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Northpointe Bancshares, Inc. (NPB) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Charles Williams, Founder, Chairman & CEO, highlighted Northpointe’s growth since its IPO, stating, "Prior to the IPO, we ended 2024 with total assets at $5.2 billion. Today, I'm proud to report that we've grown to over $7 billion in total assets, driven by tremendous growth in our Mortgage Purchase Program or MPP business." He noted a 15% increase in earnings per diluted share to $2.11 for 2025, and a tangible book value per share rise of 13.9% on an annual basis. Williams emphasized, "We've delivered robust balance sheet growth and consistent earnings throughout 2025. This was driven by sustained momentum and strengthened results across each of our key business lines while maintaining a strong credit and compliance culture."
  • The CEO underlined strong performance in MPP, with balances up over $1.7 billion year-over-year, and a 20% growth rate in All In One loans. Residential mortgage originations increased by 20% to $2.5 billion for 2025.
  • Kevin Comps, President & Secretary, explained, "Average balances [in MPP] increased by over $410.2 million from the prior quarter. Period ending balances increased by $60.1 million over the prior quarter, which is in line with our guidance." He reported $457.0 million in MPP balances participated out to partner banks by year-end. Comps also mentioned the addition of four new MPP clients in the quarter and a $234.2 million increase in savings and money market deposits from a new digital deposit relationship.
  • Bradley Howes, Executive VP & CFO, stated, "For the fourth quarter of 2025, we had net income to common stockholders of $18.4 million or $0.52 per diluted share." He highlighted the completion of a capital stack optimization by replacing preferred stock with subordinated debt, resulting in "$3.2 million or $0.09 per share in additional expense from the unamortized deal issuance costs."

Outlook

  • Howes projected a 2026 net interest margin range of 2.45% to 2.55%. MPP loan balances are expected to reach between $4.1 billion and $4.3 billion by year-end, with $300 million to $500 million on average participated out. He also forecast period ending AIO balances of $900 million to $1.0 billion by year-end 2026, and the remainder of the loan portfolio declining to $1.9 billion to $2.1 billion.
  • Howes guided for total saleable mortgage originations of $2.2 billion to $2.4 billion in 2026, with all-in margins of 2.75% to 3.25%. MPP fees are expected to rise to between $9 million and $11 million, with loan servicing revenue forecast in the same range.
  • Total noninterest expense for 2026 is projected at $138 million to $142 million, with the increase "more than offset by the growth in total revenue based on the positive operating leverage we have been able to generate."

Financial Results

  • Net income to common stockholders for Q4 2025 was $18.4 million or $0.52 per diluted share, with an adjusted $0.61 per share excluding certain expenses. Net interest income grew by $3.2 million from the prior quarter, reflecting a $393.2 million increase in average interest-earning assets and a 4 basis point improvement in net interest margin.
  • MPP balances increased by $60.1 million over Q3, net of $457 million in participations. AIO loan balances rose by $31 million. Total assets reached $7.0 billion at year-end, up from $6.8 billion in Q3.
  • Noninterest income fell $2.4 million from the prior quarter due to lower gain on sale revenue, partially offset by higher MPP and loan servicing fees. Excluding certain fair value items, net gain on sale of loans was $16.6 million, slightly down from Q3.
  • Noninterest expense decreased by $581,000 from the prior quarter, primarily due to lower salaries and benefits.

Q&A

  • Crispin Love, Piper Sandler: Asked about the impact of recent mortgage rate declines on 2026 guidance. Howes responded that the impact was "pretty minimal... We need to see kind of a more sustained decline to really see a significant benefit to our P&L." Comps added that Q1 2026 volume should be higher than Q1 2025 based on the current rate environment.
  • Love: Inquired about assumed mortgage rates for 2026. Howes said, "Rates dipping to below 6% towards the end of the year, but sort of a slow drip over the course of the year."
  • Love: Asked about net interest margin trajectory. Howes stated, "You'll see a little bit of continued improvement... but then we have 2 rate cuts that get embedded in the middle part of the year, where we see a little bit of a decline."
  • Damon Del Monte, KBW: Sought color on reserve level and provision guidance. Howes explained, "If you exclude MPP... we're probably about 37 basis points of coverage to the HFI book." He said the reserve level is expected to decline as portfolio mix improves.
  • Del Monte: Asked about rising taxes and insurance expense. Howes said it would "continue to go up," primarily due to higher FDIC insurance charges.
  • Christopher Marinac, Janney Montgomery Scott: Probed the digital deposit relationship and its sustainability. Comps said, "We did partner with an online platform... We're continuing to look for opportunities like that."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed a neutral tone, focusing on clarification of guidance, cost trends, and funding strategies, without pressing skepticism or negative sentiment.
  • Management’s tone was confident in prepared remarks and remained consistent during Q&A, with frequent references to "encouraged" trends and the expectation of "continued improvement."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, management maintained a stable, confident tone, and analysts continued to probe for additional detail on growth initiatives and cost dynamics without signaling heightened concern.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for 2026 remained in the same net interest margin range (2.45% to 2.55%) but now incorporates two expected Fed rate cuts, versus the four rate cuts forecasted previously.
  • Strategic focus on digital deposit and custodial account expansion intensified, with a $234.2 million increase in digital deposits in Q4, compared to the previous quarter’s $306.9 million custodial deposit onboarding.
  • Analysts continued to focus on margin, reserve levels, and funding mix, while management provided more detailed breakdowns of loan participations and digital deposit initiatives.
  • Key metrics such as total assets, MPP balances, and net income increased sequentially, while noninterest expense showed a modest decline.
  • Management’s confidence in loan quality and portfolio mix improvement persisted, and overall sentiment from both management and analysts was consistent with the prior quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management identified asset quality as a central risk, but reported no systemic credit or borrower issues and cited a "normal migration of credit trends on the seasoned loan portfolio."
  • Net charge-offs rose to $1.2 million, with the charge-off ratio remaining well below historical averages; management attributed these to isolated occurrences and noted sufficient collateral coverage.
  • Noninterest income volatility, specifically in gain on sale revenue, was noted as a challenge, though partially offset by MPP and servicing fees.
  • The rising cost of FDIC insurance and the sensitivity of digital deposits to interest rates were acknowledged as ongoing concerns, mitigated by efforts to diversify funding sources and manage deposit betas.

Final Takeaway

Northpointe Bancshares emphasized that robust growth in the Mortgage Purchase Program and digital deposit strategies have driven a significant increase in total assets and profitability, with forward-looking expectations for continued expansion in MPP balances and revenue streams in 2026. Management remains confident in the strength of loan quality and disciplined cost control, while maintaining a focus on growing core deposits and further optimizing the capital structure to support ongoing shareholder value.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: F/m Investments seeks SEC approval for tokenized TBIL shares
data.description:
F/m Investments on Wednesday filed with the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission to allow ownership of shares of its U.S. Treasury three-month bill exchange-traded fund (TBIL) on a permissioned blockchain ledger.

If the exemptive application gets approved, the requested relief would allow TBIL's (TBIL) existing ETF shares to be represented on a blockchain ledger under the same CUSIP, with the same rights, fees, voting rights and economic terms as TBIL shares today, the $18B investment firm said in a statement.

This new approach would allow TBIL to support traditional brokerage rails and digital-native, token-aware platforms through a single share class. It wouldn't, however, change what the fund holds or how it trades.

"Tokenization is coming to securities markets whether we file this application or not," said Alexander Morris, CEO of F/m Investments. "The question is whether it happens inside the regulatory framework investors have relied on for 85 years, or without that set of protections for investors. We'd rather build an on-ramp that marries technological innovation and investor protection than watch from the sidelines."

Tokenized shares are traditional securities—from stocks to bonds—whose ownership is represented and recorded as tokens on a blockchain ledger, rather than solely in traditional books-and-records systems. F/m's application is a test of whether on-chain plumbing can work inside the existing rules.

data.title: Old National outlines 4% to 6% loan growth for 2026 while strengthening capital position
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Old National Bancorp (ONB) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Jim Ryan, CEO & Chairman, opened by highlighting "strong fourth quarter earnings, marking an exceptional year that set new organizational records for adjusted earnings per share, net income and the efficiency ratio." He emphasized a focus on core deposit growth, positive operating leverage, disciplined credit management, and robust liquidity and capital ratios. He noted the successful completion of the Bremer Bank partnership integration, describing it as "one of our smoothest and most successful integrations ever."
  • Ryan stated, "driving tangible book value per share growth is a key priority," reporting a 15% increase in tangible book value per share for the year, despite merger-related charges and the repurchase of 2.2 million shares.
  • Looking forward, Ryan confirmed an ongoing strategy of balancing organic capital building with share repurchases: "We will maintain the right balance between building capital organically and returning capital through share repurchases."
  • John Moran, Senior Executive VP & CFO, reported, "fourth quarter 2025 was a strong finish to a highly successful year, marked by records in adjusted EPS and efficiency with peer-leading profitability, improvement in already durable credit metrics and significant capital generation despite closing Bremer." He further noted, "GAAP 4Q earnings per share of $0.55" and "adjusted earnings per share were $0.62, a 5% increase over the prior quarter and a 27% increase year-over-year."

Outlook

  • The company projects 1Q 2026 loan growth of 3% to 5% and full year loan growth of 4% to 6%. Management stated, "We anticipate continued success in the execution of our deposit strategy and expect to meet or exceed industry growth in 2026 and generally in line with our asset growth."
  • Fee income is expected to remain strong, supported by favorable rate environments in mortgage and capital markets. Expense guidance incorporates the full run rate on Bremer cost savings, with management expecting "full year results that yield significant growth in earnings per share and again feature positive operating leverage with a peer-leading return profile."
  • The company assumes two 25 basis point rate cuts in 2026 and expects "noninterest-bearing deposits to remain relatively stable."

Financial Results

  • Adjusted earnings per share for the quarter were $0.62. Net interest income and margin both increased, with "modest margin expansion...supported by deposit repricing."
  • Tangible book value per share increased by 4% from Q3, totaling a 15% increase over the year. Total loans grew 6.4% annualized from the prior quarter, and the loan-to-deposit ratio reached 89%.
  • Noninterest income was $126 million for the quarter, with management highlighting "better-than-expected performance within mortgage and capital markets."
  • Adjusted noninterest expenses were $365 million for the quarter, and the adjusted efficiency ratio reached a record low of 46%.
  • Credit quality improved, with an 8% reduction in criticized and classified loans and a 12% decrease in nonaccrual loans. The allowance for credit losses to total loans stood at 124 basis points.

Q&A

  • Robert Siefers, Piper Sandler: Asked about margin trajectory and the bigger step-up in net interest income in the second half of the year. John Moran responded that "the bigger factor...is actually day count," with margin drivers being growth, curve steepness, asset repricing, and deposit beta management.
  • Siefers: Inquired about the pace of share repurchases. CEO Ryan indicated, "we plan to be more active than we were last year."
  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde Group: Questioned margin stability after adjusting for day count. Moran confirmed, "a day count adjusted margin is stable, if not a bit grinding higher."
  • Nosal: Asked about reserve coverage ratio. Moran attributed lower provision to "the migration in the criticized and classified book and improvement on those measures."
  • Jared Shaw, Barclays: Sought clarity on CET1 targets. Moran said, "very comfortable with where we are on CET1 today...we don't view it as mutually exclusive to grow a little bit of capital and return capital in 2026."
  • Ben Gerlinger, Citi: Asked about growth sources. Timothy Burke, President & COO, cited "broad-based growth from the C&I middle market" and emphasis on talent investment.
  • Terence McEvoy, Stephens: Inquired about fee income outlook and mortgage yields. Moran expressed "cautiously optimistic" views on mortgage for 2026 and described new loan and securities yields as offering incremental benefits.
  • Sun Young Lee, TD Cowen: Asked about securities portfolio yields and deposit costs. Moran indicated the securities portfolio mix will stay stable and sees room to lower exception price book rates.
  • Christopher McGratty, KBW: Asked about deposit pricing and technology investment. Moran described deposit pricing as "very, very rational" and Ryan noted, "we are not underfunding new investments...we're really good at self-funding a lot of that."
  • David Chiaverini, Jefferies: Queried about loan growth and borrower sentiment. Burke observed "customers are feeling more optimistic about 2026" and highlighted middle market C&I as a growth driver.
  • Jon Arfstrom, RBC: Asked about ongoing strategic portfolio management. Moran said it is "constant and ongoing."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts maintained a constructive but probing tone, focusing on margin sustainability, capital management, and growth strategies, with a generally neutral to slightly positive sentiment.
  • Management’s tone in prepared remarks was confident, reinforced by repeated references to "peer-leading profitability" and successful integration. In Q&A, management remained assured, addressing questions on buybacks, margins, and growth with specific details and reiterating commitment to organic growth and capital returns.
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts' tone remained consistent, while management exhibited increased confidence due to operational milestones and strong financial results.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The guidance language has evolved from stable to improving, as management now targets 4% to 6% loan growth for 2026, up from the prior quarter's 4% to 5% (excluding Bremer). Fee income guidance remains optimistic, and the efficiency ratio improved from below 50% to a record low of 46%.
  • Strategic focus remains on organic growth and capital returns, with a more active share buyback program signaled for 2026. The Bremer integration, discussed as ongoing in Q3, is now complete and described as highly successful in Q4.
  • Analysts’ questions continued to center on margin, capital, and loan growth, with additional focus on integration benefits and technology investment in Q4.
  • Management's confidence was higher, emphasizing successful integration, peer-leading returns, and robust capital generation compared to the prior quarter.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted seasonality in public funds as a minor headwind for core deposits, noting "seasonally lower public funds balances."
  • Credit risk was mitigated through active portfolio management, evidenced by consistent declines in criticized and classified loans.
  • Management reaffirmed that exposure to nondepository financial institutions remains "de minimis."
  • Analysts probed potential competitive pressures in loan pricing and deposit costs, and management responded by underscoring disciplined pricing models and rational market behavior.

Final Takeaway

Old National Bancorp capped an exceptional year with record profitability, strong loan and deposit growth, and a successful Bremer integration. Management projects 4% to 6% loan growth in 2026, supported by active talent investment and a disciplined approach to capital management, while maintaining a focus on organic growth and peer-leading operating metrics.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Wall Street rallies higher after Trump speaks at Davos
data.description:
Wall Street trades higher on Wednesday after President Donald Trump eased market concerns about a U.S. invasion to secure Greenland.

The tech-focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was last higher by 1.7%. At the same time, the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was up by 1.5%, and the blue-chip Dow (DJI) had gained 1.5%. 

From a sector vantage point, all of the S&P 500’s 11 sectors were in positive trading territory with energy leading the way. In reverse, real estate has been the weakest performer. 

Treasury yields were mostly even with the shorter-end U.S. 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) moved down 1 basis point to 3.58%, while the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) was lower by 4 basis points to 4.25%. 

Trump said at the WEF in Davos that he was seeking immediate negotiations for the acquisition of Greenland from Denmark and doesn't want to use excessive force. 

“Stocks are reacting to tariff fears for the first time in months, which is especially notable since many market participants thought stocks had become desensitized to tariff fears. We remind investors that tariff headlines can cause short-term volatility, but that applies in both directions. The tariff threats can easily be unwound and reversed, sparking upside market volatility,” David Laut, chief investment officer at Kerux Financial stated. 

Top S&P 500 gainers included Intel (INTC) +11.1% and United Airlines (UAL) 2.5%. Decliners included Kraft Heinz (KHC) -6.5%, Netflix (NFLX) -2.5%.

data.title: APA, CNX Resources on the rise even as Barclays cuts to Sell equivalent
data.description:
APA Corp. (APA) and CNX Resources (CNX) were downgraded Wednesday to Underweight from Equal Weight with respective $24 and $34 price targets at Barclays, but the stocks trade +3.6% and +2.4%, respectively, as energy names rise across the board.

APA (APA) was one of the clearer examples of conventional assets re-rating against a de-rating of U.S. shale, Barclays analyst Betty Jiang said, now estimating Permian Basin standalone free cash yield at just 3.8% in 2026 at strip prices, a significant premium to larger U.S. onshore pure play peers with greater scale and inventory quality.

Also, APA's (APA) outsized gas marketing gains - which contributed the bulk of corporate free cash flow in 2024- 25 - are set to compress materially as Permian offtake expansions narrow Waha dislocations and global LNG prices weaken compared to the U.S., the analyst wrote.

Shares of CNX Resources (CNX) trade roughly at par with gas E&Ps on unlevered free cash flow yield despite meaningfully shorter inventory duration, with inventory credit already ascribed to the deep Utica, a play that remains far from fully de-risked, Jiang said.

Barclays also reinstated Occidental Petroleum (OXY) with an Equal Weight rating following the closing of the OxyChem divestiture, which Jiang said will accelerate debt reduction below levels targeted after the CrownRock acquisition, but at cost of a free cash flow vehicle that provided some insulation to the energy commodity cycle.

Moving into 2026, Jiang likes Ovintiv (OVV), seeing the Anadarko Basin divestiture that completes its transformation into a Permian-Montney dual-focused liquids play as a clear catalyst to re-rate the company; EQT (EQT), which the analyst believes the market has not sufficiently differentiated the structural growth optionality and margin advantage afforded by midstream integration in gas; and Viper Energy (VNOM), which remains one of the better
vehicles for Permian exposure at a discounted valuation.

data.title: Birkenstock gains after Trump backs away from the latest European tariff threat
data.description:
Birkenstock Holding plc (BIRK) rallied in Wednesday afternoon trading after the Trump Administration called off the most recent European tariff threats related to the U.S. pursuit of Greenland. Earlier in the day, President Trump had backed off some of his weekend rhetoric about the U.S. aiming to take over Greenland.

Shares of Birkenstock (BIRK) are sensitive to tariff developments because tariffs directly pressure the company's gross margins and earnings outlook. Management has quantified the impact of tariffs on earnings several times in the past. The Germany-based company’s European manufacturing base and heavy U.S. exposure mean any change in U.S.–EU tariff policy can materially alter its cost of goods, pricing strategy, and growth algorithm.

Birkenstock (BIRK) has passed on some tariff costs to customers while absorbing the rest as a hit to margins.

Shares of Birkenstock (BIRK) were up 5.1% in late Wednesday afternoon trading. The footwear is down more than 30% over the last 52 weeks. Wall Street analysts and Seeking Alpha analysts both have a consensus Buy rating on BIRK.

data.title: Wall Street jumps as Trump says he won’t impose Greenland tariffs on Feb. 1
data.description:
The markets (SP500), (COMP:IND), (DJI) responded with elevated enthusiasm on Wednesday’s afternoon trade after U.S. President Donald Trump announced on Truth Social that he will not be imposing the tariffs scheduled to go into effect Feb. 1.

“Based upon a very productive meeting that I have had with the Secretary General of NATO, Mark Rutte, we have formed the framework of a future deal with respect to Greenland and, in fact, the entire Arctic Region,” Trump said.

“Based upon this understanding, I will not be imposing the Tariffs that were scheduled to go into effect on February 1st. Additional discussions are being held concerning The Golden Dome as it pertains to Greenland,” he added.

Earlier on Wednesday, European leaders announced they had suspended a trade agreement with the U.S. as Trump continued his push to acquire Greenland during a speech at the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland.

The S&P 500 (SP500) was last 1.5% on afternoon trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was 1.5% as well, and the Dow (DJI) was 1.6%.

Investors can follow more on Wednesday’s market moves here.

data.title: F.N.B. outlines mid-single-digit loan and deposit growth for 2026 as balance sheet surpasses $50B
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: F.N.B. Corporation (FNB) Q4 2025

Management View

  • Vincent J. Delie, Chairman, President & CEO, stated that FNB reported fourth quarter operating net income available to common shareholders of $182 million or $0.50 per diluted common share, with full year operating net income of $577 million and operating earnings per diluted share of $1.59. Delie highlighted, "Full year operating EPS grew 14% year-over-year, driven by the 9% growth in net interest income, significant margin expansion and record noninterest income." He emphasized the company's focus on resetting the balance sheet, managing loan concentrations, and improving the loan-to-deposit ratio to 89.7%. Delie also announced the transfer of approximately $200 million of performing residual mortgage loans to held for sale, expected to close in Q1 2026.

  • Delie highlighted the digital strategy: "Our comprehensive digital strategy, including our early adoption of AI, remains a driving force behind client acquisition, engagement and convenience." The introduction of a payment switch feature and plans for additional digital capabilities were discussed as part of FNB's Clicks-to-Bricks strategy.

  • Delie noted, "Through our disciplined expense management culture, FNB has achieved annual cost savings of $10 million to $20 million per year since 2019," and projected even higher cost savings in 2026 through automation and process improvements.

  • Gary L. Guerrieri, Chief Credit Officer, reported strong asset quality: "Total delinquency ended the quarter at 71 basis points, up 6 bps from the prior quarter with NPLs and OREO down 6 bps, ending at a multiyear low of 31 basis points." He added, "Net charge-offs totaled 19 basis points and 20 basis points for the year, showing continued strong performance throughout an uncertain economic environment."

  • Vincent J. Calabrese, Chief Financial Officer, stated, "Fourth quarter operating net income totaled a record $181.8 million or $0.50 per share when excluding a discretionary $20 million charitable contribution to the FNB Foundation partially offset by a reduction in the estimated FDIC special assessment." He also said, "Record total revenues of nearly $458 million, grew a very strong 12.4% on an operating basis and operating pre-provision net revenue grew 21.5% from the year ago quarter."

Outlook

  • Calabrese provided 2026 guidance: "For full year 2026, period-end loans and deposits are expected to grow mid-single digits versus year-end 2025 as we continue to increase our market share across our diverse geographic footprint." Net interest income for 2026 is expected to be between $1.495 billion and $1.535 billion, with Q1 net interest income projected between $355 million and $365 million. Noninterest income is guided to be between $370 million and $390 million for the year, and noninterest expense between $1 billion and $1.02 billion. Provision expense is expected between $85 million and $105 million for 2026.

  • Delie reiterated, "Looking ahead to 2026, we are confident in our ability to deliver meaningful loan and deposit growth, margin expansion and further diversification of fee income."

Financial Results

  • FNB's total assets at year-end 2025 exceeded $50 billion for the first time. Fourth quarter average loans and leases were $35 billion, up $169 million from last quarter. Average deposits were $38.6 billion, an increase of $740 million linked quarter annualized.

  • Net interest income for Q4 was $365.4 million, up 1.7% from the prior quarter. Noninterest income reached $92.3 million, with wealth management revenues up 15% year-over-year. The net interest margin was 3.28%, up 3 basis points from Q3.

  • Operating noninterest expense totaled $256.5 million, with an efficiency ratio of 53.8%, 307 basis points better than Q4 2024.

  • FNB's capital levels remained at record levels with a CET1 ratio at 11.4% and tangible common equity ratio at 8.9%.

Q&A

  • Daniel Tamayo, Raymond James: Asked about fee income guidance for 2026 and drivers for achieving upper-end guidance. Calabrese said, "We had all-time highs for 7 of our fee-based businesses for the full year and 4 of them in the fourth quarter alone... The newer businesses that we've talked about starting to contribute at higher levels is definitely baked into the guidance."

  • Tamayo followed up on operating leverage. Calabrese noted, "Our guidance includes a meaningful increase in PPNR and in the operating leverage... expenses growing in the low single digits, while we're continuing to invest in the new initiatives."

  • Russell Gunther, Stephens: Inquired about loan growth, particularly in C&I and CRE. Delie responded, "The C&I production was extraordinarily good... we used '25 to kind of position our company to grow CRE loans and to grow C&I loans more rapidly."

  • Gunther also asked about CET1 targets. Calabrese: "Dividend payout for the full year, then you combine that with our expectation for strong internal capital generation... we're in the best position we've ever been to deploy capital to optimize shareholder value."

  • Casey Haire: Asked about deposit beta trends and credit provision outlook. Calabrese: "I would say 30% or so by the end of the year... still kind of a mid-30s once this cycle finishes." Guerrieri confirmed provision guidance assumptions.

  • Kelly Motta, KBW: Asked about buyback price sensitivity and M&A. Calabrese: "We think our stock is worth a lot more than where it's trading... there's not a bright line where we would stop."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts pressed for specifics on loan growth, capital deployment, and efficiency ratios, with a neutral to slightly positive tone. Questions focused on the sustainability of performance and potential for increased capital returns.

  • Management maintained a confident and optimistic tone, emphasizing strong momentum, strategic digital investments, and readiness for growth. Delie said, "We are very pleased with our financial results and achieved a number of records for 2025 including revenue, noninterest income and EPS."

  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts remained focused on growth and efficiency, while management's confidence appeared even more pronounced, especially regarding digital initiatives and capital flexibility.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for loan and deposit growth shifted from projecting toward the lower end of mid-single digits in Q3 to a firm mid-single-digit expectation for 2026.

  • Management's tone increased in confidence around technology deployment, cost savings, and operating leverage, while analysts continued to focus on capital returns and loan growth.

  • Key metrics such as CET1 ratio, tangible book value, and net interest margin all improved quarter-over-quarter.

  • Strategic focus shifted further toward digital banking, AI, and automation as drivers of efficiency and growth.

Risks and Concerns

  • Guerrieri noted ongoing monitoring of line utilization and industry concentrations, but reported continued positive credit migration and no material impacts from tariffs since Q1.

  • Delinquency and NPLs remained low, and management emphasized strong credit risk practices and capital strength as mitigation strategies.

  • Analysts questioned loan pricing competitiveness and the sustainability of efficiency gains, with management stressing discipline and selectivity in lending and expense management.

Final Takeaway

FNB closed 2025 with record revenue, strong profitability, and a balance sheet exceeding $50 billion in assets. Management highlighted a robust outlook for 2026, projecting mid-single-digit loan and deposit growth, further margin expansion, and continued investment in digital capabilities and automation. The company’s capital strength and disciplined credit management underpin its confidence in pursuing growth opportunities while maintaining operational efficiency and flexibility for shareholder returns.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: MRNA is S&P 500’s top performing stock, here are the best Quant pharma companies
data.description:
The pharmaceutical sector continues to attract investor attention, with recent clinical developments highlighting the industry’s innovation potential.

Moderna (MRNA) emerged as the top-performing pharmaceutical stock in the S&P 500 (SP500) on Wednesday after announcing that its mRNA-based cancer therapy, intismeran autogene, combined with Merck’s (MRK) Keytruda, demonstrated a 49% reduction in the risk of tumor recurrence or death over five years in a late-stage melanoma trial.

The following stock screen focuses on companies categorized within the pharmaceuticals sector (PJP), (XPH), (PPH). The ranking is determined by Seeking Alpha’s advanced Quant Rating system, which evaluates stocks based on multiple quantitative factors designed to predict future performance potential.

The Quant Rating system categorizes stocks across a spectrum from “Strong Buy” to “Hold” based on various metrics. Stocks receiving a Strong Buy rating generally score above 4.5, while Buy ratings fall between approximately 3.5 and 4.5, and Hold ratings typically range from around 3.0 to 3.5. The stocks presented below represent the highest-rated pharmaceutical companies according to these quantitative metrics.

Here are the top pharma stocks by Quant ratings. Interestingly, Moderna (MRNA) is not among the top 15:

  1. Indivior PLC (INDV) - SA Quant rating: 4.94 (Strong Buy) (YTD: -6.42%)
  2. Liquidia Corporation (LQDA) - SA Quant rating: 4.93 (Strong Buy) (YTD: 13.99%)
  3. Sol-Gel Technologies Ltd. (SLGL) - SA Quant rating: 4.93 (Strong Buy) (YTD: 71.02%)
  4. Pfizer Inc. (PFE) - SA Quant rating: 4.89 (Strong Buy) (YTD: 1.31%)
  5. Oramed Pharmaceuticals Inc. (ORMP) - SA Quant rating: 4.86 (Strong Buy) (YTD: 20.14%)
  6. Astellas Pharma Inc. (ALPMY) - SA Quant rating: 4.86 (Strong Buy) (YTD: 4.01%)
  7. Merck & Co., Inc. (MRK) - SA Quant rating: 4.82 (Strong Buy) (YTD: 8.27%)
  8. Bristol-Myers Squibb Company (BMY) - SA Quant rating: 4.73 (Strong Buy) (YTD: 0.06%)
  9. Innoviva, Inc. (INVA) - SA Quant rating: 4.68 (Strong Buy) (YTD: -2.20%)
  10. Amneal Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (AMRX) - SA Quant rating: 4.50 (Strong Buy) (YTD: 6.05%)
  11. Theravance Biopharma, Inc. (TBPH) - SA Quant rating: 4.30 (Buy) (YTD: 8.67%)
  12. ANI Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (ANIP) - SA Quant rating: 4.14 (Buy) (YTD: 3.52%)
  13. Septerna, Inc. (SEPN) - SA Quant rating: 4.06 (Buy) (YTD: -8.62%)
  14. Phibro Animal Health Corporation (PAHC) - SA Quant rating: 4.04 (Buy) (YTD: 10.28%)

data.title: Apple's weakness is a 'buying opportunity' into earnings: Goldman
data.description:
The recent weakness in Apple's (AAPL) shares — down seven weeks in a row — is a “buying opportunity” ahead of the iPhone creator's quarterly earnings, Goldman Sachs said.

Goldman Sachs analyst Michael Ng said he expects Apple to earn $2.66 per share for the coming quarter, with iPhone revenue estimated to grow 13% year-over-year and shipments up 5% over the same time frame, including 26% growth in China. Ng has a Buy rating and a $320 price target on Apple.

“iPhone demand in the next 2 years is likely to benefit from the iPhone Fold (Fall 2026, 4.5/25.4M units in F2026/F2027), the shift to a biannual iPhone launch cycle with the iPhone 18 base and iPhone Air 2 delayed from Fall 2026 to Spring 2027, and new software upgrades with iOS and Siri 2.0,” Ng wrote in a note to clients. “Although App Store spending decelerated again in F1Q26E to +7% [year-over-year], Services revenue growth (GSe: +14% [year-over-year]) should be supported by momentum in other categories (e.g., TAC, iCloud+, AppleCare+) with further tailwinds in F2026 from new ad formats in the App Store.”

Ng added the price and mix growth of Apple's products and the continued shift towards services should help boost gross margins, even if memory costs rise. Additionally, the company's partnership with Google (GOOG) (GOOGL) for Siri and continued iPhone demand ”should demonstrate to the market that the iPhone will remain the consumer device of choice for accessing new AI tools, clearing overhangs related to competition.”
